name: Rongo
description: Rongo, also known as Lono in some Polynesian cultures, is a prominent deity associated with agriculture, peace, fertility, and the growth of cultivated plants. He is considered one of the four great gods in some Polynesian traditions, alongside Tangaroa, Tūmatauenga, and Tāne. Rongo's origins are tied to the creation myths, where he played a crucial role in separating Ranginui (sky father) and Papatūānuku (earth mother), allowing light and life to enter the world. This act, while beneficial, also led to the separation of the divine parents and the subsequent struggles among their offspring. Rongo is often depicted as a benevolent and nurturing figure, responsible for the abundance of crops and the overall well-being of the land. He is invoked in rituals and ceremonies related to planting, harvesting, and ensuring a plentiful food supply. His influence extends to the arts and festivities associated with the agricultural cycle, emphasizing the importance of peace and prosperity within the community. In some narratives, Rongo is also associated with healing and the restoration of harmony, reflecting his connection to the natural world and its regenerative powers. Offerings of the first fruits of the harvest were traditionally made to Rongo to express gratitude for his blessings and ensure continued abundance. He represents the vital connection between humanity and the earth, highlighting the importance of sustainable practices and respect for the environment.
subtitle: God of Agriculture, Peace, and Fertility

keyMyths.title: Separation of Rangi and Papa
keyMyths.description: Rongo, along with his brothers, conspired to separate Ranginui and Papatūānuku, bringing light to the world. This act, while necessary for life, caused great sorrow to the divine parents.

keyMyths.title: Origin of the Kumara
keyMyths.description: Rongo is credited with bringing the kumara (sweet potato) to the islands, a staple crop that sustained many Polynesian communities. This gift symbolized Rongo's benevolence and commitment to providing sustenance.
keyMyths.source: Orbell, Margaret. The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend.

keyMyths.title: Rongo's Association with Peace
keyMyths.description: Unlike his brother Tūmatauenga, the god of war, Rongo is strongly associated with peace and diplomacy. His influence promotes harmony and cooperation within communities, ensuring prosperity for all.
